questions.question: What was the main objective of the Permanent Settlement?
questions.answer: To secure a fixed and regular land revenue for the British government.

questions.question: How did the Permanent Settlement change the role of zamindars?
questions.answer: It made zamindars responsible for collecting revenue and paying it to the British.

questions.question: Why did many zamindars lose their estates under British rule?
questions.answer: They failed to pay the fixed land revenue demanded by the British.

questions.question: What was the impact of auctioning zamindari lands?
questions.answer: New landlords such as merchants and moneylenders emerged.

questions.question: Why did peasants often fall into debt during colonial rule?
questions.answer: Because of heavy taxes and high-interest loans from moneylenders.

questions.question: Why was indigo cultivation profitable for European planters?
questions.answer: Indigo produced blue dye used in European textile industries.

questions.question: What was the system of advances in indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: Planters gave loans that forced peasants to grow indigo.

questions.question: Why did indigo cultivation harm peasants’ farming?
questions.answer: It reduced soil fertility and limited food crop production.

questions.question: Why were peasants reluctant to grow indigo?
questions.answer: It provided little profit and damaged their land.

questions.question: What was one major cause of the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: Forced indigo cultivation by European planters.

questions.question: How did peasants resist indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: They refused to grow indigo and protested against planters.

questions.question: What role did moneylenders play in rural society?
questions.answer: They provided loans but often trapped peasants in debt.

questions.question: Why did colonial policies encourage cash crops?
questions.answer: Cash crops generated profit for British trade.

questions.question: How did indigo cultivation affect food production?
questions.answer: Land used for indigo reduced the area for food crops.

questions.question: What action did the British government take after the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: It set up the Indigo Commission.

questions.question: Why was the Indigo Commission established?
questions.answer: To investigate the grievances of peasants.

questions.question: How did colonial land policies transform rural society?
questions.answer: They created new landlords and increased economic pressure on peasants.

questions.question: Why did European planters dominate indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: They controlled the factories and export trade.

questions.question: What economic problem did peasants face under colonial rule?
questions.answer: They faced poverty and heavy debt.

questions.question: How did colonial policies exploit Indian agriculture?
questions.answer: They forced farmers to grow cash crops for export.

questions.question: Why did peasants depend on moneylenders?
questions.answer: They needed money to pay taxes and farming expenses.

questions.question: How did the Indigo Revolt reflect peasant resistance?
questions.answer: Peasants united to oppose exploitation by planters.

questions.question: What does the Indigo Revolt reveal about colonial agriculture?
questions.answer: It shows how colonial policies exploited farmers.

questions.question: Why was indigo cultivation unpopular among peasants?
questions.answer: It damaged soil and provided low income.

questions.question: Why is the Indigo Revolt significant in Indian history?
questions.answer: It represents organized resistance against colonial exploitation.
